text: Returning to running at 48 hours post-marathon does not negatively impact muscle damage recovery (measured by CK and LDH) up to eight days post-race compared to complete rest.

Active recovery via running at moderate intensity (95-105% of first ventilatory threshold HR) for 40 minutes at 48, 96, and 144 hours post-marathon enhances neuromuscular performance (squat jump) at 96 hours compared to passive rest.
Elliptical training as active recovery post-marathon does not provide a significant neuromuscular recovery advantage over passive rest, unlike running-based active recovery.
Post-marathon active recovery sessions should be performed at moderate intensity around the first ventilatory threshold for approximately 40 minutes to aid neuromuscular recovery.
Muscle damage markers (CK and LDH) follow the same time course of recovery regardless of whether active or passive recovery is used post-marathon.
Neuromuscular recovery (squat jump performance) post-marathon can be accelerated by resuming running 48 hours after the race, with a large effect size compared to rest.
